steps.title: Estimate, then quantify uncertainty
steps.body: Point estimates summarize a population with one number, but samples vary. This chapter covers standard errors, margins of error, confidence intervals, correct interpretation, and sample-size planning.

definitions.definition: A single best-guess value for a population parameter (e.g., x̄ estimates μ).
The sampling variability of an estimate: SE = σ ÷ √n.
How far the estimate may be off: MOE = z* × SE.
A range, estimate ± MOE, likely to contain the true parameter.
The long-run share of such intervals that capture the parameter (e.g., 95%).
The multiplier that sets the confidence level (1.96 for 95%).

steps.title: Identify a point estimate
steps.context: A sample of 64 customers reports average wait time of 12.4 minutes at a coffee shop.
steps.prompt: What is the point estimate for the population mean wait time?
hints: A point estimate is a single sample statistic used to estimate a parameter.
For population mean, the point estimator is sample mean x-bar.

feedback.wrong: Use the sample mean as the point estimate for population mean.

solution.body: The point estimate is x-bar = 12.4 minutes.
solution.takeaway: Point estimates are sample statistics standing in for unknown parameters.

steps.title: Compute standard error
steps.context: Assume population SD sigma = 12 and sample size n = 36.
hints: SE for sample mean is sigma/sqrt(n).

solution.takeaway: Standard error measures sampling variability of the estimator.

steps.title: Where z* = 1.96 comes from
steps.prompt: A 95% confidence interval keeps the middle 95% of the normal sampling distribution. Drag the bounds to about z = −2 and z = +2 (the exact cutoffs are ±1.96) and watch the shaded area approach 95%. Those cutoffs are the z* you multiply by the standard error.

feedback.ready: The central ~95% sits between z ≈ ±1.96 — that's why z* = 1.96 for 95% confidence. Want 99%? Widen the bounds (z* ≈ 2.58). Want 90%? Narrow them (z* ≈ 1.645).

steps.title: Margin of error
steps.context: For a 95% CI with known sigma: z* = 1.96, sigma = 10, n = 100.
steps.prompt: Find the margin of error (MOE = z* x sigma/sqrt(n)).

feedback.wrong: Multiply z* by the standard error.

solution.takeaway: Confidence intervals are estimate plus/minus a margin of error.

steps.title: Build interval endpoints
steps.context: A sample mean is x-bar = 52 with margin of error 1.96.
steps.prompt: What is the lower endpoint of the interval (x̄ − MOE) when x̄ = 52 and MOE = 1.96?

feedback.wrong: Subtract the margin of error from 52.

solution.takeaway: Always anchor interval endpoints at the estimate before adding/subtracting MOE.

steps.title: Confidence interval simulator
steps.prompt: Run repeated samples and watch 95% confidence intervals either capture or miss the true mean.

feedback.ready: A 95% method captures the true mean about 95% of repeated samples, not every single interval.

steps.title: Correct CI interpretation
steps.context: You computed a 95% confidence interval for a population mean.

options.label: 95% of individual observations lie in this interval
There is a 95% chance this fixed interval contains mu
Using this method repeatedly, about 95% of intervals will contain mu

hints: Confidence level describes the long-run success rate of the procedure.
After data are collected, the interval is fixed; the parameter is fixed too.

feedback.wrong: Interpret confidence as repeated-sampling capture rate.

solution.body: The right interpretation is long-run method performance: about 95% of similarly constructed intervals capture mu.
solution.takeaway: Confidence belongs to the method, not to one realized interval as a probability statement about mu.

steps.title: How sample size changes precision
steps.context: Suppose all else is unchanged and confidence level stays at 95%.
steps.prompt: If sample size quadruples, what happens to margin of error?

feedback.wrong: Use the square-root relationship between n and MOE.

solution.body: MOE is halved because MOE proportional to 1/sqrt(n) and sqrt(4n) = 2sqrt(n).
solution.takeaway: Precision improves slowly: big gains in n create moderate gains in MOE.

steps.title: Plan required sample size
steps.context: Target a 95% CI with margin of error at most 2. Assume sigma = 12 and z* = 1.96.
steps.prompt: What minimum sample size n is required?

feedback.wrong: Compute n from the MOE formula, then round up to the next whole number.

solution.takeaway: Always round sample-size calculations up to guarantee the target precision.

steps.title: Polling estimate
steps.context: A poll of n = 400 voters finds 54% support for a proposal.
steps.prompt: What is the point estimate of true support p?
hints: For a population proportion, use sample proportion p-hat.
p-hat is the observed percent in decimal form.

feedback.wrong: The point estimate for p is p-hat from the sample.

solution.takeaway: Capstone starts from the sample statistic that estimates the target parameter.

steps.title: Proportion margin of error
steps.context: Use a 95% interval for p with p-hat = 0.54 and n = 400.
steps.prompt: Approximate the margin of error (MOE = 1.96 x sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n)).

feedback.wrong: Use the 95% proportion MOE formula with p-hat and n.

solution.takeaway: For proportions, uncertainty depends on both p-hat and sample size.

steps.title: Decision from the interval
steps.context: From parts above, a 95% CI is approximately 0.54 +/- 0.05, or [0.49, 0.59].
steps.prompt: What conclusion is justified about majority support (> 50%)?

options.label: No clear majority conclusion because 0.50 is inside the interval (0.49 to 0.59)

hints: Check whether 0.50 lies inside or outside the confidence interval.
If the null benchmark is inside the interval, evidence is not decisive at that confidence level.

feedback.wrong: Use interval inclusion: if 0.50 is inside, majority support is not confirmed.

solution.body: Because 0.50 lies inside [0.49, 0.59], the data do not conclusively show majority support at the 95% level.
solution.takeaway: Capstone flow: estimate, compute MOE, build CI, and judge claims by whether benchmark values are excluded.

steps.title: Chapter 6 complete
steps.body:
You built estimation workflows from point estimates to confidence intervals, margin of error, and sample-size planning.

You can now move from raw samples to defensible statistical claims with quantified uncertainty.

review.title: Margin of error
review.prompt: Compute the margin of error z* × (σ / √n).

feedback.wrong: Multiply z* by the standard error σ / √n.

solution.takeaway: Margin of error = z* × standard error.

review.title: Reading a confidence interval
review.context: A 95% confidence interval for a mean is **[12, 18]**.
review.prompt: Is 20 a plausible value for the true mean at this level?

options.label: A CI can't tell you that

hints: A value is plausible only if it lies inside the interval.

feedback.wrong: Only values inside the interval are plausible at that level.

solution.body: 20 lies **outside** [12, 18], so it is not a plausible value at the 95% level.
solution.takeaway: A CI is the range of plausible parameter values; outside it is implausible.

review.title: Interval endpoint
review.context: A sample mean **x̄ = 50** with margin of error **4**.
review.prompt: What is the upper endpoint of the confidence interval?

feedback.wrong: Add the margin of error to the estimate.

solution.takeaway: A confidence interval is estimate ± margin of error.
